finding.contract_claim: L532-533: The `_PARSERS` dict maps `setup.cfg` and `Pipfile` to `_parse_requirements_txt` with comments noting the parser misses INI/TOML structure. These are accurate limitation notes, but `discover_manifests` includes both files via `_MANIFEST_FILES`, meaning callers will receive silently incomplete results for these formats without any warning at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Either implement proper parsers for `setup.cfg` (INI `[options] install_requires`) and `Pipfile` (TOML `[packages]`), or emit a warning when these manifests are parsed so callers know coverage is partial.
finding.triage_reasoning:
**Reality — gap exists.**

L528-533 of `_PARSERS`:
```python
"setup.cfg": _parse_requirements_txt,  # best-effort: only matches bare pkg==ver lines, misses INI structure
"Pipfile":   _parse_requirements_txt,  # best-effort: only matches bare pkg lines, misses TOML structure
```
Both filenames are also in `_MANIFEST_FILES` (L272-279), so `discover_manifests` returns them to the pipeline. In `detect_dead_deps_async` (L788+), each discovered manifest is read and passed to `parse_manifest`, which dispatches through `_PARSERS`. For `setup.cfg` and `Pipfile` that dispatch lands on `_parse_requirements_txt`.

`_parse_requirements_txt` does line-by-line `re.match(r"([A-Za-z0-9][\w.\-]*)", line)` after skipping blank lines, comments, and `-r`/`--` directives. A canonical `setup.cfg` body looks like:
```
[options]
install_requires =
    requests
    flask>=2
```
The section header `[options]` starts with `[`, which matches the leading `[A-Za-z0-9]` pattern, producing a spurious candidate named `options`. The actual package names (`requests`, `flask`) sit on indented lines and would also match by accident, but the structural intent (INI group membership) is entirely lost — any key under a non-`install_requires` section gets silently included, and multi-line values after the `=` may be parsed as package names. A `Pipfile` with TOML syntax (`[packages]\nrequests = "*"`) similarly provides no structure, causing both section headers and value strings to be misinterpreted.

**The code's own comments acknowledge this**: "misses INI structure", "misses TOML structure". No runtime warning is emitted — confirmed by grepping all `warning`/`warn`/`logger`/`LOG` hits in the file; the only `[warn]` prints are for LLM resolution and small-model classification failures, not for parser coverage gaps.

**Significance — closing the gap improves correctness.**
A repo using `setup.cfg` or `Pipfile` will produce quietly wrong coverage: some packages silently missed (false "no deps found"), some false positives injected from INI/TOML structural tokens. No caller signal exists that results are partial. Widening the gap (e.g., adding more file types routed to the wrong parser) would further corrupt audit results.

**Actionability — concrete fix available.**
Two options, both concrete:
1. *Short-term*: emit a `print(f"  [warn] {manifest_path}: parsed as plain-text requirements (INI/TOML structure ignored — coverage may be incomplete)", flush=True)` inside `parse_manifest` when the filename is `setup.cfg` or `Pipfile`. This matches the existing `[warn]` convention already used at L843 and L887.
2. *Proper fix*: implement `_parse_setup_cfg` (use `configparser` to read `[options] install_requires`) and `_parse_pipfile` (use `tomllib`/`tomli` already imported for `pyproject.toml`) and update `_PARSERS` accordingly.
